result:
Dawn folds itself over the harbor like a secret just learned. Gulls scribble white arcs across lavender sky, while a lone ferry yawns awake, engine turning diesel dreams into motion. I stand at the edge of a splintered pier with bare feet tasting salt, convinced the water remembers every footstep that ever dared its skin. Tiny mussels clamp to barnacled ribs of the dock, small fists insisting on permanence.  

In the parking lot behind me, a trumpet lies abandoned on the hood of a faded Civic; no one has come to claim it, and the dew beads along its brass veins like apologies too late. I press a fingertip to the mouthpiece, half expecting warmth, but it’s only cold metal telling stories I can’t quite translate. Perhaps it was played at midnight while drunk lovers slow-danced on the concrete, or maybe it’s a relic from someone who thought music could rebuild a broken bridge. Either way, its silence hums louder than a chord.  

Far out, the first sun spear strikes the ferry’s windshield, igniting a glare that flings gold all the way back to my eyes. I blink, seeing sudden constellations even the night forgot. Somewhere in that burst of light lies an alphabet no longer bound to apology. So I write this without caution, the way gulls write wind, the way boats write distance, the way trumpets write absence when no one listens.
